response:
### Part 1: General Solution for the Population Model

The differential equation provided is:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = rP \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) + A \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right)P
\]
Substituting the given values \( r = 0.3 \), \( K = 1000 \), and \( A = 0.1 \):
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = 0.3P \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)P
\]
Rearranging the terms to group the population factor \( P \):
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) \right]
\]
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) - \frac{0.3}{1000}P \right]
\]
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ \underbrace{0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)}_{a(t)} - \underbrace{\frac{0.3}{1000}}_{b}P \right]
\]
This equation is a **Bernoulli differential equation** of the form \( P' + b(t)P = c(t)P^n \) (specifically \( n=2 \)) or, more directly, a time-dependent logistic equation. To solve it, we use the substitution \( u = \frac{1}{P} \), which implies \( \frac{du}{dt} = -\frac{1}{P^2} \frac{dP}{dt} \).

Dividing the original equation by \( P^2 \):
\[
\frac{1}{P^2} \frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{1}{
